Claude responded: Approved — dark-glassmorphism with that accent choice is exactly right.
Validated design direction and charted next architectural steps
Validated design direction and charted next architectural steps
Approved — dark-glassmorphism with that accent choice is exactly right. Space Grotesk/Outfit against Inter or Geist gives us the "opinionated but readable" balance we want, and spring-physics + ease-out-expo is the right motion vocabulary for this to feel alive rather than just decorated. Lock that in as the direction.
One thing I want to flag before we go further, though — looking at the files touched (Hero.tsx, Navbar.tsx, Projects.tsx, page.tsx), it looks like we jumped straight into building page sections. That's actually a bit ahead of where Phase 0 wants us to be. Here's my concern architecturally:
If Hero, Navbar, and Projects get built now, each one will likely hardcode its own color values, spacing, blur amounts, and easing curves inline. Then when we formalize the design token system afterward, we're not applying tokens — we're retrofitting three components to use them, which almost always leaves inconsistencies (a blur here that doesn't match a blur there, an accent color hardcoded in one spot vs. referencing a CSS variable in another). That's precisely the kind of drift that separates a premium-feeling site from one that just has premium-looking pieces.
So before we treat any of those section components as final, I'd like us to back up slightly and do this in the right order:
Design tokens as CSS variables — color scale (including the glass surface colors and border-white/5-style overlay tokens), fluid type scale, spacing scale, motion tokens (durations + easing curves named and centralized, e.g. --ease-out-expo, --spring-snappy).
Primitive components — Button, Container, Section, Badge, and a GlassPanel/Surface primitive (since glassmorphism surfaces will be reused constantly — navbar, cards, modals — this deserves its own primitive rather than being redefined per component).
A quick /style-guide route that renders all of these primitives together, so we can see the whole system cohesively before a single real section is built on top of it.
Then Hero, Navbar, and Projects get built (or refactored) to consume that system rather than invent their own values — meaning less rework, not more, in the long run.
Question for you: did those four files already establish something like a tokens file (globals.css / tailwind.config extensions) underneath them, or were the values hardcoded directly in the components? If tokens already exist, we can skip straight to the primitives + style guide. If not, let's start there — go ahead and set up the token layer first, and show me the /style-guide route once the primitives are wired to it.

